   
   
   
## ![](data:image/png;base64,)  
**📝Challenge Scenario**  
The corporate network of Quick Logistics LLC has come under a stealthy cyberattack that slipped past the initial defenses. Suspicious user activity triggered alerts pointing to potential lateral movement and credential dumping. As a security analyst, your task is to investigate how the attacker maintained persistence, escalated privileges using UAC bypass, extracted credentials via Mimikatz, and eventually targeted the domain controller with a DCSync attack. Using ELK stack and Sysmon logs, this incident response journey reveals every stage of compromise — from the first payload to the final ransomware deployment.  
## **🧩Challenge Overview**  
The **Boogeyman 3** challenge simulates a multi-stage cyberattack on  **Quick Logistics LLC**, beginning with a phishing email sent to the  **CEO, Evan Hutchinson**. Though  **Evan** reported the suspicious email, the attacker had already executed a stage-one payload hidden inside an  **ISO file** (review.dat). The payload was copied to a stealthy location and triggered using  **rundll32.exe**, establishing persistence through a scheduled task named  **“Review.”** Exploiting a  **UAC bypass** via  **fodhelper.exe**, the attacker escalated privileges and used a  **GitHub-hosted Mimikatz** tool to dump credentials. Armed with the credentials of a local admin ( **itadmin**), the attacker accessed a PowerShell script ( **IT_Automation.ps1**) from a shared drive, revealing domain credentials ( **allan.smith:Tr!ckyP@ssw0rd987**). These were used to move laterally to another host ( **WKSTN-1327**), where further credentials were dumped, including the hash of the  **local administrator**. The final stage saw the attacker perform a  **DCSync attack** from the  **Domain Controller**, dumping the hashes of privileged accounts and completing a  **full domain compromise**.  
## **🩻TASK 2 The Chaos Inside**  
Before I dive into the investigation, I make sure to **set the right time range** in Kibana. The attacker activity happened between  **August 29, 2023 @ 00:00:00.0** and  **August 30, 2023 @ 23:30:00.0**, so I adjust the filter to only include logs within this window. This helps me avoid noise and focus only on what matters. Without setting this, I might miss out on key events or waste time going through irrelevant data. Filtering by time is always my  **first step**.  
Press enter or click to view image in full size  
![](data:image/png;base64,)  
## **🆔1.) What is the PID of the process that executed the initial stage 1 payload?**  
I started by filtering **Event ID 1** in ELK. This event type is important because it logs the creation of new processes, which is exactly what I need to trace the start of the attack chain.  
I then focused on any suspicious execution of **powershell.exe**, since attackers often use PowerShell for payload delivery. While digging through the logs, I found that the parent process was  **mshta.exe**, a known LOLBin🗑️often abused in attacks. Looking at the image below, I could confirm this by checking the field process.parent.name, which shows mshta.exe.  
Press enter or click to view image in full size  
![](data:image/png;base64,)  
Right below that, the field process.parent.pid displays the PID as **6392**, which means  **this is the process that executed the initial stage 1 payload**. This PID helps track the attacker's movement across the system and is critical for chaining together the rest of the behavior.  
### **😺1st Flag: 6392**  
The PID of the process that executed the initial stage 1 payload is **6392**, confirming that mshta.exe was the launching point of the attack chain.  
## **👹2.) The stage 1 payload attempted to implant a file to another location. What is the full command-line value of this execution?**  
I first filtered for Windows-native tools that could indicate file movement especially commands like copy or xcopy. That’s when I spotted the process xcopy.exe, which caught my attention🤓.  
Press enter or click to view image in full size  
![](data:image/png;base64,)  
Looking deeper, I confirmed it was used to implant the payload by transferring the file from D:\review.dat to a new path. The full command-line clearly showed.  
### **😺2nd Flag: “C:\Windows\System32\xcopy.exe” /s /i /e /h D:\review.dat C:\Users\EVAN~1.HUT\AppData\Local\Temp\review.dat**  
This confirmed that the **stage 1 payload** attempted to implant itself into the user’s temporary folder to maintain stealth and persistence.  
## **📁3.) The implanted file was eventually used and executed by the stage 1 payload. What is the full command-line value of this execution?**  
To trace the execution of the implanted file, I searched for any usage of review.dat, which was previously copied into a temporary directory. I then investigated the process tree and found that fodhelper.exe was invoked, commonly used in **UAC bypass** techniques due to its auto-elevated privileges.  
Press enter or click to view image in full size  
![](data:image/png;base64,)  
Upon checking the parent process details of fodhelper.exe, I found the following parent command line.  
Press enter or click to view image in full size  
![](data:image/png;base64,)  
### **😺3rd Flag: “C:\Windows\System32\rundll32.exe” D:\review.dat,DllRegisterServer**  
This confirmed that the attacker executed the malicious DLL file (review.dat) using **rundll32.exe**, passing the DllRegisterServer export as an argument which is a way to execute DLL payloads.  
## **💣4.) The stage 1 payload established a persistence mechanism. What is the name of the scheduled task created by the malicious script?**  
To begin my investigation, I filtered the logs using the filename projectfinancial* and narrowed it further with winlog.event_id: 1 to only view process creation events. This helped reduce noise and zone in on suspicious script-based executions.  
Press enter or click to view image in full size  
![](data:image/png;base64,)  
Among the logs, I saw a powershell.exe execution referencing review.dat, which I initially thought was a harmless file. But on deeper inspection, I noticed it was actually being passed as an argument to rundll32.exe, which is commonly abused to load DLLs. This made it clear that review.dat was a malicious DLL.  
Press enter or click to view image in full size  
![](data:image/png;base64,)  
The PowerShell command used New-ScheduledTaskAction along with other task scheduling functions, which indicated the attacker was trying to set up a **persistence mechanism** on the machine. Inside that command, I discovered the scheduled task name created by the attacker.  
### **😺4th Flag: Review**  
The attacker established persistence by creating a scheduled task named **Review**, which executed the malicious review.dat DLL.  
## **🤖5.) The execution of the implanted file inside the machine has initiated a potential C2 connection. What is the IP and port used by this connection? (format: IP:port)**  
To investigate this, I already knew from earlier steps that the **malicious DLL file** (disguised as review.dat) was executed using rundll32.exe. Since  **rundll32.exe** ** is the process responsible for executing the payload**, I focused on its potential network behavior.  
I began by filtering for **network-related logs**, specifically  **winlog.event_id: 3**, which tracks  **network connections**. Then, I filtered using the process name rundll32.exe* to find if any outgoing connections were established during or after its execution.  
Press enter or click to view image in full size  
![](data:image/png;base64,)  
While scrolling through the logs, I identified a log entry showing a **network connection** initiated by rundll32.exe. The log revealed a connection attempt to the  **IP address ** **165.232.170.151** ** over port ** **80**.  
Press enter or click to view image in full size  
![](data:image/png;base64,)  
### **😺5th Flag: 165.232.170.151:80**  
The rundll32.exe payload successfully initiated a Command-and-Control (C2) connection to 165.232.170.151 over port 80.  
## **👤6.) The attacker has discovered that the current access is a local administrator. What is the name of the process used by the attacker to execute a UAC bypass?**  
I filtered for **fodhelper.exe** under  **Windows Event ID 1** because this ID is commonly associated with process creations. I suspected this process might have been used by the attacker for privilege escalation.  
![](data:image/png;base64,)  
Below, I found a record showing **fodhelper.exe** being used to run a command that executes  **rundll32.exe** with the malicious  **review.dat** DLL. This confirmed that the attacker used  **fodhelper.exe** to bypass UAC and elevate privileges to execute the DLL file.  
Press enter or click to view image in full size  
![](data:image/png;base64,)  
To verify this, I searched online and confirmed that **fodhelper.exe** is a known method used in  **UAC bypass** techniques. The attacker exploited this trusted process to run malicious commands without triggering the User Account Control prompt.  
Press enter or click to view image in full size  
![](data:image/png;base64,)  
### **😺6th Flag: fodhelper.exe**  
The UAC bypass was achieved using **fodhelper.exe**, a legitimate Windows executable often abused for privilege escalation.  
## **⚒️7.) Having a high privilege machine access, the attacker attempted to dump the credentials inside the machine. What is the GitHub link used by the attacker to download a tool for credential dumping?**  
While researching credential dumping, I found that **Mimikatz** is one of the most commonly used tools by attackers. This tool allows attackers to extract plaintext credentials from LSASS memory, perform  **pass-the-hash**, and forge  **Kerberos tickets**. Understanding this helped narrow down what to look for when searching for evidence of credential dumping in the logs.  
Press enter or click to view image in full size  
![](data:image/png;base64,)  
I decided to use **mimikatz*** as my search term to find any indication of the tool being used or downloaded. This allowed me to locate PowerShell activity and command-line arguments related to Mimikatz.  
![](data:image/png;base64,)  
From the log, I discovered that PowerShell was used to fetch a ZIP file from the following URL.  
Press enter or click to view image in full size  
![](data:image/png;base64,)  
This is a clear indication that the attacker downloaded **Mimikatz** from GitHub directly to the compromised system, likely to extract user credentials.  
### **😺7th Flag: **[ **https://github.com/gentilkiwi/mimikatz/releases/download/2.2.0-20220919/mimikatz_trunk.zip**](https://github.com/gentilkiwi/mimikatz/releases/download/2.2.0-20220919/mimikatz_trunk.zip "https://github.com/gentilkiwi/mimikatz/releases/download/2.2.0-20220919/mimikatz_trunk.zip")  
The attacker downloaded **Mimikatz** via PowerShell from GitHub to perform credential dumping.  
## **#️⃣8.) After successfully dumping the credentials inside the machine, the attacker used the credentials to gain access to another machine. What is the username and hash of the new credential pair? (format: username:hash)**  
To begin investigating whether the attacker used credentials from a dumped session, I used this filter.  
![](data:image/png;base64,)  
This helped narrow down the logs to execution events (Event ID 1) involving the tool mimikatz, which is commonly used for credential dumping.  
The filtered result shows two relevant logs.  
Press enter or click to view image in full size  
![](data:image/png;base64,)  
- **First log (bottom):** Shows a PowerShell command being used to download mimikatz from GitHub.  
- **Second log (top):** Occurred shortly after and shows the execution of mimikatz.exe.  
Here, mimikatz.exe is highlighted again, and we can clearly see the **path to the executable**, as well as a suspicious execution command.  
Press enter or click to view image in full size  
![](data:image/png;base64,)  
This command specifically uses **pass-the-hash (pth)** technique with the provided  **NTLM hash**, targeting the itadmin user which typically a privileged account.  
   
After that to further investigate I click into that specific query and check the message section.  
Press enter or click to view image in full size  
![](data:image/png;base64,)  
In this message section of the event log:  
- The OriginalFilename confirms the tool used: **mimikatz.exe**  
- The full command line shows the **user: ** **itadmin**  
- The **NTLM hash** used is: F84769D250EB95EB2D7D8B4A1C5613F2  
Together, this proves the attacker used the dumped credential (itadmin with the provided hash) to try accessing another system.  
### **😺8th Flag: itadmin:F84769D250EB95EB2D7D8B4A1C5613F2**  
By filtering for mimikatz executions and analyzing the event logs, I identified that the attacker used the credential itadmin along with a dumped **NTLM** hash, confirming lateral movement using high-privilege credentials.  
## **🗃️9.) Using the new credentials, the attacker attempted to enumerate accessible file shares. What is the name of the file accessed by the attacker from a remote share?**  
I began by filtering events using a simple and effective query.  
Press enter or click to view image in full size  
![](data:image/png;base64,)  
This helped me narrow down any command-line activity that could point toward file enumeration using either cmd.exe or powershell.exe.  
While going through the results, I found a suspicious use of PowerShell.  
Press enter or click to view image in full size  
![](data:image/png;base64,)  
This showed that the attacker accessed a **PowerShell script file from a remote share**, which immediately raised a red flag for me.  
To confirm the filename, I checked the full command line again.  
Press enter or click to view image in full size  
![](data:image/png;base64,)  
This proved that the attacker used PowerShell to read a script directly from the ITFiles share of a remote machine named WKSTN-1327.  
### **😺9th Flag: IT_Automation.ps1**  
I successfully identified that the attacker used their escalated access to browse shared drives and opened the file **IT_Automation.ps1** from a remote system using PowerShell.  
## **🪪10.) After getting the contents of the remote file, the attacker used the new credentials to move laterally. What is the new set of credentials discovered by the attacker? (format: username:password) ?**  
To determine the new credentials used by the attacker during lateral movement, I started by continuing my log investigation using the same filter query.  
Press enter or click to view image in full size  
![](data:image/png;base64,)  
While reviewing the command line activity logs, a specific event caught my attention because it contained the use of the $Credential variable a strong indicator that credential usage or manipulation was occurring.  
Press enter or click to view image in full size  
![](data:image/png;base64,)  
Upon inspecting this event further, I discovered a suspicious PowerShell command that constructs a PSCredential object using a hardcoded username and a secure string conversion of a plaintext password. The command was structured to impersonate a user when executing remote commands on a target machine.  
Press enter or click to view image in full size  
![](data:image/png;base64,)  
This clearly reveals that the attacker had harvested and reused a set of credentials to initiate lateral movement. The username and password pair was embedded directly in the script and used to remotely access the system WKSTN-1327.  
### **😺10th Flag: QUICKLOGISTICS\allan.smith:Tr!ckyP@ssw0rd987**  
This confirms that allan.smith's credentials were obtained by the attacker and leveraged to access another host within the network.  
## **🎯11.) What is the hostname of the attacker’s target machine for its lateral movement attempt?**  
To determine the hostname of the machine targeted during the attacker’s lateral movement, I continued examining the same suspicious PowerShell event from the previous step. This event contained the hardcoded credentials (QUICKLOGISTICS\allan.smith:Tr!ckyP@ssw0rd987) and also revealed the destination hostname embedded within the PowerShell command.  
In the message section of the event, the attacker used the Invoke-Command cmdlet with the -ComputerName parameter to remotely execute a script on another host. The computer name specified was.  
Press enter or click to view image in full size  
![](data:image/png;base64,)  
This clearly indicates that the attacker used the compromised credentials to target and attempt remote code execution on this machine.  
### **😺11th Flag: WKSTN-1327**  
This hostname represents the attacker’s next point of lateral movement in the compromised network.  
## **👨‍💻12.) Using the malicious command executed by the attacker from the first machine to move laterally, what is the parent process name of the malicious command executed on the second compromised machine?**  
I started by filtering for events specifically related to the targeted machine using the following query.  
Press enter or click to view image in full size  
![](data:image/png;base64,)  
This allowed me to focus only on process creation events from the second machine involved in the lateral movement.  
Press enter or click to view image in full size  
![](data:image/png;base64,)  
This caught my attention because wsmprovhost.exe is commonly used by PowerShell Remoting, which aligns with lateral movement activity.  
### **😺12th Flag: wsmprovhost.exe**  
To confirm, I checked the parent process of this execution and found that wsmprovhost.exe was indeed the parent process used during the remote execution initiated from the compromised first machine.  
## **#️⃣13.) The attacker then dumped the hashes in this second machine. What is the username and hash of the newly dumped credentials? (format: username:hash)**  
I started by refocusing my investigation on credential dumping activity. To achieve this, I used the following filter.  
![](data:image/png;base64,)  
This filter is designed to capture any process execution related to Mimikatz, a known tool often used by attackers to extract passwords and hashes from memory.  
While scanning through the results, I spotted an event that grabbed my attention. The command line contained a reference to the user parameter and Mimikatz execution path.  
Press enter or click to view image in full size  
![](data:image/png;base64,)  
This strongly indicated a pass-the-hash attack using Mimikatz. I analyzed the event further by checking both the process.parent.args and process.command_line fields for verification.  
Press enter or click to view image in full size  
![](data:image/png;base64,)  
From the command arguments, I identified the dumped username and NTLM hash:  
- **Username:** administrator  
- **Hash:** 00f80f2538dcb54e7adc715c0e7091ec  
### **😺13th Flag: administrator:00f80f2538dcb54e7adc715c0e7091ec**  
This clearly shows that the attacker successfully extracted and used the administrator credentials to further their access.  
## **🥟14.) After gaining access to the domain controller, the attacker attempted to dump the hashes via a DCSync attack. Aside from the administrator account, what account did the attacker dump?**  
To answer this, I started by filtering for any suspicious DCSync activity using the following search query.  
![](data:image/png;base64,)  
This helped me locate potential Mimikatz-based attacks targeting domain controller synchronization.  
From the filtered results, I found an event where mimikatz.exe was executed from the path.  
Press enter or click to view image in full size  
![](data:image/png;base64,)  
This immediately caught my attention due to the explicit /user: field, indicating a targeted DCSync hash extraction.  
I further examined the process.command_line field and confirmed that the attacker had executed.  
Press enter or click to view image in full size  
![](data:image/png;base64,)  
This confirmed that in addition to the administrator account, the attacker attempted to dump the password hash for the backupda user.  
### **😺14th Flag: backupda**  
This user account was the additional target in the DCSync operation conducted using Mimikatz.  
## **☠️15.) After dumping the hashes, the attacker attempted to download another remote file to execute ransomware. What is the link used by the attacker to download the ransomware binary?**  
To identify any attempts by the attacker to download a ransomware payload, I began by focusing on the suspicious process name I had previously noticed: ransomboogey.exe. Using this clue, I filtered the event logs with the following query.  
![](data:image/png;base64,)  
This search yielded a few events of interest. I examined each of them carefully and eventually came across a command in one of the PowerShell events that stood out. The command was using Invoke-WebRequest (iwr) to fetch a remote file.  
Press enter or click to view image in full size  
![](data:image/png;base64,)  
The script downloads the file and saves it locally as ransomboogey.exe in the user directory of *evan.hutchinson*. This strongly confirms the attacker's intent to deploy ransomware post-credential dumping and lateral movement.  
Press enter or click to view image in full size  
![](data:image/png;base64,)  
### **😺15th Flag: **[ **http://ff.sillytechninja.io/ransomboogey.exe**](http://ff.sillytechninja.io/ransomboogey.exe "http://ff.sillytechninja.io/ransomboogey.exe")
